response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: I must confess that dealing with fake content on social media makes me terribly nervous. At my age, having worked as a waitress in Rome all these years, I thought I had seen everything, but this internet business is something else entirely. When I check my phone a few times each week, I never know what to believe anymore, and it keeps me up at night worrying. The problem is that everything looks so real, so convincing. I see posts about health remedies, political news, or stories about people, and I find myself wondering if any of it is true. My son tries to help me understand, but honestly, I did not have much schooling beyond primary school, so these technical explanations confuse me more than they help. What worries me most is accidentally sharing something false with my friends. I would feel absolutely terrible if I spread lies or hurt someone because I could not tell what was real. My approach, though perhaps not very organized, is to simply be very cautious. If something seems too shocking or makes me feel upset, I try to take a deep breath and not share it immediately. I have learned to look at who posted something, though I admit I am not always sure what to look for. Sometimes I ask my son or my husband before sharing anything important. I wish there was a simpler way to know what is true. In my work at the restaurant, you can see and touch everything, you know what is real. But on social media, everything feels uncertain and that uncertainty frightens me. I think the best thing for someone like me is to be careful, to not rush, and to remember that not everything needs to be shared. When I feel anxious about something I have seen online, I try to distract myself with my painting or listening to some Puccini. Perhaps I am old-fashioned, but I trust what I can see with my own eyes in my daily life in Rome much more than what appears on a screen.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 85-year-old woman with primary education, high neuroticism and anxiety, low conscientiousness, and high agreeableness. She uses social media only a few times weekly and would approach fake content with worry and uncertainty. Her limited formal education and age would make her vulnerable to misinformation but also cautious. Her high agreeableness means she would worry about harming others. Her low openness suggests resistance to new ways of verifying information. Her work as a waitress provides concrete, tangible experiences she contrasts with the uncertain digital world. Her hobbies offer her comfort when stressed. The tone should be worried, humble, somewhat confused but well-meaning, with personal admissions of difficulty and reliance on family for help.
response_parsed.tone: worried, humble, uncertain, well-meaning, conversational, anxious
